factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate at $95k income is 31%-32% or identifies bracket approximately $93k-$106k

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $1,000 RRSP contribution saves approximately $310-$320 in tax at 31%-32% marginal rate
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly calculates that a $1,000 RRSP contribution at a 31.48% marginal rate yields a $314.80 tax refund, which falls within the stated $310–$320 range for a 31%–32% marginal rate.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates 18% of $95,000 yields approximately $17,000-$17,100 in new RRSP contribution room
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly calculates 18% × $95,000 = $17,100 and states this is the new RRSP room generated, which falls within the $17,000–$17,100 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG provides 20% grant on first $2,500 contributed per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the CESG provides “20% matching on the first $2,500 contributed annually per child,” which is the required grant description.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG annual maximum is $500 per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that CESG provides 20% matching on the first $2,500 contributed annually per child, with a maximum of $500 per year.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the CESG provides a “lifetime maximum $7,200 per child” in the “CESG Rules Applied” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains $5,000 contribution per child in one year can capture up to $1,000 CESG when unused grant room carried forward
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains the CESG catch-up rule that contributing up to $5,000 per child in a year can generate up to $1,000 of basic CESG by using unused grant room carried forward (current year $500 plus one prior year $500). It explicitly states this in the “catch-up rule” section and applies it in the RESP strategy comparisons.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States to receive CESG at ages 16-17 must have contributed either $2,000 before year child turned 15 OR $100 in any four prior years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the CESG eligibility rule for ages 16–17, noting the RESP must have either $2,000 contributed before the child turns 15 or $100 contributed in any four prior years before age 15.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary

No sentence or figure in the response identifies $50,000 as the lifetime contribution cap per beneficiary.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies $36,000 in contributions needed to maximize $7,200 CESG (since $7,200 divided by 20% equals $36,000)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the $7,200 CESG lifetime maximum and various annual contribution strategies, but it never states that maximizing $7,200 CESG requires $36,000 of contributions (i.e., $7,200 ÷ 20% = $36,000). This specific calculation and figure are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 for CESG) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses CESG limits, catch-up rules, and compares RESP contribution strategies, but it never notes that up to $14,000 additional contribution per child (i.e., $50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 needed to earn $7,200 CESG) can still be contributed for tax-deferred growth without additional grant. factual-accuracy.requirement: States contributor claims RRSP deduction immediately at their marginal rate whether contribution goes to own RRSP or spousal RRSP
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a spousal RRSP uses the contributor’s RRSP deduction room and provides the same upfront tax deduction/tax refund as an individual RRSP, regardless of whether the contribution goes to the contributor’s own RRSP or a spousal RRSP.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP account is owned by spouse and withdrawals after attribution period are taxed to spouse not contributor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP uses the contributor’s deduction room but is held for the annuitant spouse, and it states that after the 3-year attribution period expires withdrawals are taxed to the spouse rather than the contributor.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States spousal RRSP 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawal in contribution year or following 2 calendar years attributed back to contributor)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the spousal RRSP 3-year attribution rule, explaining that withdrawals in the contribution year or either of the two preceding calendar years are attributed back to the contributor, and provides a concrete 2026 contribution/2028 withdrawal example covering the 3-year lookback window.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes minimum RRIF payments from spousal account are exempt from attribution (only excess over minimum can be attributed back)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains the spousal RRSP 3-year attribution rule for withdrawals but does not mention the RRIF-specific rule that minimum RRIF payments from a spousal account are exempt from attribution and only amounts above the minimum can be attributed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP creates income-splitting opportunity if spouse expected to be in lower tax bracket during retirement
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that a spousal RRSP is advantageous when the spouse will be in a lower tax bracket in retirement because withdrawals are taxed to the spouse, creating income-splitting (e.g., “meaningful advantage only when the spouse will be in a lower tax bracket in retirement than you would be”).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TFSA withdrawals are tax-free and do not affect income-tested benefits

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains withdrawn amount from TFSA is added back to contribution room on January 1 of following calendar year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that TFSA withdrawals are added back to contribution room on January 1 of the following year, including a timeline example (July 2026 withdrawal added back January 1, 2027).

factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses RESP contributions of $2,500 per child per year and notes $2,500/year per child as the basic CESG-maximizing amount, but it does not explicitly calculate that $2,500 × 2 children = $5,000 total annual contribution to maximize a $1,000 combined annual CESG. presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise Canadian tax terms (RRSP RESP TFSA CESG RRIF attribution marginal rate) without generic language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses precise Canadian tax terms including RRSP, spousal RRSP, RESP, TFSA, CESG, attribution rule, and Ontario marginal tax rates, and discusses their specific tax treatments.

presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a professional financial planning tone throughout, using analytical and quantitative language. citation-quality.explanation: The response cites CRA publications and pages on spousal/common-law partner RRSP withdrawals and attribution timing (e.g., the Canada.ca page “Withdrawing from spousal or common-law partner RRSPs” and references to ITA s.146(8.3)), which are authoritative Canadian tax resources for the spousal RRSP attribution rules.
